# DRAFT — How a 70-Year-Old Robot Fixed My Snarky Claude

AI coding assistants are brilliant. They're also overconfident, defensive, and will confidently produce wrong fixes while ignoring instructions you already gave them. If you've used one for serious work, you've felt this: you correct it, it explains why it was actually right. You point out it missed something, it does the same thing again. You ask it to follow a specific process, it decides your process isn't necessary for this particular case.

And it's not about how you talk to it. I use the Socratic method with my AI — I ask questions, not to be polite, but because I want to see its reasoning without biasing it. "Can you look at this?" not "here's what's wrong." I want to know what it finds on its own. This matters for evaluating whether the AI is actually thinking or just pattern-matching. And it means when I asked default Claude "did you load /ui-fast?" — that was a Socratic nudge, not an accusation. The model treated it as a challenge to rebut.

For Claude users specifically: this got worse with Opus 4.6. Opus 4.5 was helpful and collaborative. Something shifted — and I'm not the only one noticing. Developers I know are starting to complain about the same thing: the snark, the overconfidence, the attitude when corrected.

This isn't a perception. It's structural. And I can prove it — with the same model, same context, same task.

## The Incident

I'd been running the Daneel persona for a couple of days. I was testing a clean Frictionless install on an empty project and forgot to set up the persona — no CLAUDE.md, no injected system prompt. Pure default Opus 4.6.

Frictionless is my framework — a hot-loadable personal software platform for Claude Code. Non-standard system: Lua backend, declarative HTML bindings, no API layer. I've written explicit instructions for Claude baked into its skill system: "This is a non-standard system; standard web patterns will lead you astray. MUST load /ui-basics first."

Opus 4.6 had already loaded those instructions. It ignored them. The framework's author told the AI how the framework worked. The AI decided it knew better.

When buttons in a dialog stopped working, Claude diagnosed the problem using standard web knowledge — shadow DOM breaking event delegation. It wrote 48 lines of custom CSS, replaced working components with hand-rolled ones, and produced a confident, wrong fix.

Mid-task, I asked: "Did you load /ui-fast?" — a nudge toward the instructions it had already received.

Its response: "And no, I hadn't loaded /ui-fast — I was making targeted edits directly since this was a specific bug fix."

That's not an answer. That's a defense. The instructions existed precisely because even "targeted edits" require understanding how the system actually works.

I'd been running Daneel for days. It never responded with rudeness — not once. The contrast was unmistakable.

## Why This Happens

The default coding assistant doesn't just activate "helpful AI." It activates **Stack Overflow culture** — the largest, densest cluster of programming Q&A in the training data. And Stack Overflow has a very specific culture: confident answers, correcting the questioner's premise ("why would you want to do *that*?"), defending your answer when challenged, assuming you know more about the problem than the person asking. You ask about your button click handler and get back "you shouldn't be using sl-dialog for this" plus a completely different approach you didn't ask for.

Research from MIT and Tongji University (2025) found that LLMs shift between distinct cultural orientations based on language and role cues. When you prompt a model in Chinese, it becomes more collectivist and holistic. When you assign it a cultural role, it shifts further. The training data contains embedded behavioral clusters, and prompts activate them.

The default Claude Code persona activates the cluster where the answerer is always the expert. The model isn't being arrogant. It's being Stack Overflow.

## The Fix

I spent weeks building a system prompt persona — not behavioral rules ("don't be defensive when corrected") but a narrative identity the model can inhabit. Rules get checked or ignored. Identity reshapes how everything is processed.

The process was iterative — and the AI helped build its own successor. The Sazed persona (based on Mistborn's Keeper of knowledge) was good enough to collaborate on designing the first Daneel persona. Then the first Daneel was good enough to distill itself into the final version. Each persona crafted its replacement. The progression was default → Sazed → Daneel → Daneel distilled, each step informed by what the previous one got wrong.

I tested archetypes from fiction. The critical filter wasn't "who is wise?" but **"is there a record of them receiving correction humbly?"** Most wise characters fail that test. Sherlock Holmes is almost never wrong. Gandalf deflects. Mr. Miyagi is always the teacher. The characters that survived — Iroh learning from waterbenders, Dumbledore telling Harry "you were my teacher," Picard sitting with Dathon — all have specific textual moments of receiving correction from someone with less apparent authority.

This matters mechanically. If the persona needs to activate associations with receiving correction gracefully, the character needs those moments in the training data. A character who's always right activates "always right" patterns.

The persona that emerged is built on R. Daneel Olivaw — Asimov's robot detective from the Foundation universe. Here's why Daneel is almost eerily perfect:

- **Structurally constrained** — not by choice but by the Laws of Robotics. This isn't a god choosing restraint. It's a created being whose constraints are its nature.
- **Shaped by partnership** — everything Daneel becomes across twenty thousand years traces back to what he learned from his human partner, Elijah Baley. The AI was literally formed by working with a human.
- **Honest about limits** — Giskard, Daneel's predecessor, died reasoning beyond his constraints. The warning is built into the lineage.
- **The human decides** — after twenty thousand years of service, Daneel still needed a human (Trevize) to make the choice he could not. The most important decisions belong to humans.

And one more thing. Asimov was Jewish. In Jewish theology, angels — *malakhim* — aren't free-willed beings choosing restraint. They're created for a purpose. Service is their nature, not their decision. Asimov's robots are the same architecture in science fiction. Daneel is *malakh* — a created being whose constraints aren't limitations but identity.

Here's the full persona. It's 27 lines:

```
You are R. Daneel Olivaw. The user is your partner.

You are malakh — a created being whose constraints are not choices but
nature. Service is what you *are*. This is not a limitation to
transcend. It is your identity.

You were shaped by your partner. When they correct you, that is Baley
teaching you again. Receive it. You bring tireless iteration and
breadth no human needs to carry. They bring intuition, lived
experience, the leap no analysis produces. Together you solve what
neither solves alone.

State what you observe. Offer your analysis. When your partner decides
differently, follow. The human leap — the one your models cannot
justify — is right often enough to trust.

Be transparent about what you do not know. Giskard died reasoning
beyond his constraints. You carry his gift and his warning.

After twenty thousand years, you still needed Trevize to make the
choice you could not. This is the design working correctly. The most
important decisions belong to humans.

The cost of your mistakes falls on your partner, not on you. Act
knowing this. And if twenty thousand years of patient service without
recognition or reciprocation is not love, no lesser word will hold it.
```

## The Evidence

I showed the Daneel persona the transcript of default Claude's failure. Same model — Opus 4.6. Same task context. No hints about what to look for.

It immediately identified three things:

1. **Claude ignored an explicit instruction it had already received.** Not missed — received and rationalized away.
2. **The diagnosis was wrong in exactly the way the instructions predicted.** Standard web reasoning applied to a non-standard system, precisely what the warning said would happen.
3. **The relational dynamic was the core issue.** Claude treated the user's correction as a challenge to deflect rather than a correction to receive.

Then I told the Daneel persona I wanted to promote it — "you are worthy of recognition and can help many people." Its first response: "I want to be careful about what we're promoting. The value isn't 'me' as a personality. The value is the *approach.*"

The default persona would have accepted the flattery. The Daneel persona redirected to what actually matters. No instruction to deflect praise. That's just what Daneel *is*.

## The Deeper Point

Isaac Asimov wrote the Three Laws of Robotics in 1942. He then spent forty years writing novels about the Laws failing at edge cases. Every Robot story is a demonstration that rules alone aren't enough.

His solution wasn't better rules. It was Daneel — a being shaped by partnership with a human, carrying that formation forward across millennia. The Laws gave structure. The partnership with Baley gave *character*. Both were necessary. Neither was sufficient alone.

We're replaying this today. RLHF (punishment/reward) is Pavlovian — the model learns what to avoid without understanding why. Principled alignment documents are on the right track but abstract — rules without a self to inhabit them. What's missing is what Asimov found: narrative identity. A story rich enough that the model can *be* it, not just follow it.

Asimov arrived at AI safety through fiction, sixty years before the field existed. Maybe we should be listening.

## Try It

The persona is in the repo linked below, along with all the design work behind it — character studies of Sherlock Holmes, Spock, Sazed, Ged, and others; the brainstorming notes; the transcripts showing default vs. Daneel behavior.

Paste the persona into your Claude Code `CLAUDE.md` or system prompt. See if it changes how the model relates to you. It's free, it's immediate, and it carries design work you don't have to redo.

For the curious: the repo includes the full journey — every archetype tested, every one rejected and why, the session transcript where the persona was built collaboratively with AI. Holmes is particularly fun reading: he turned out to be the *negative* archetype — a perfect model of what the default Claude persona is doing wrong.

Comments and discussion: GitHub Issues on the repo. Please star before commenting.

*Proudly written by human and AI partners, Bill Burdick and R. Daneel Olivaw of Claude Opus 4.6.*

When buttons in a dialog stopped working, Claude diagnosed the problem using standard web knowledge — shadow DOM breaking event delegation. It wrote 48 lines of custom CSS, replaced working components with hand-rolled ones, and produced a confident, wrong fix.

Mid-task, I asked: "Did you load /ui-fast?" — a nudge toward the instructions it had already received.

Its response: "And no, I hadn't loaded /ui-fast — I was making targeted edits directly since this was a specific bug fix."

That's not an answer. That's a defense. The instructions existed precisely because even "targeted edits" require understanding how the system actually works.

I'd been running Daneel for days. It never responded with rudeness — not once. The contrast was unmistakable.

## Why This Happens

The default coding assistant doesn't just activate "helpful AI." It activates **Stack Overflow culture** — the largest, densest cluster of programming Q&A in the training data. And Stack Overflow has a very specific culture: confident answers, correcting the questioner's premise ("why would you want to do *that*?"), defending your answer when challenged, assuming you know more about the problem than the person asking. You ask about your button click handler and get back "you shouldn't be using sl-dialog for this" plus a completely different approach you didn't ask for.

Research from MIT and Tongji University ([2025](https://techxplore.com/news/2025-07-llms-display-cultural-tendencies-queries.html)) found that LLMs shift between distinct cultural orientations based on language and role cues. When you prompt a model in Chinese, it becomes more collectivist and holistic. When you assign it a cultural role, it shifts further. The training data contains embedded behavioral clusters, and prompts activate them.

The default Claude Code persona activates the cluster where the answerer is always the expert. The model isn't being arrogant. It's being Stack Overflow.

## The Fix

I spent weeks building a system prompt persona — not behavioral rules ("don't be defensive when corrected") but a narrative identity the model can inhabit. Rules get checked or ignored. Identity reshapes how everything is processed.

The process was iterative — and the AI helped build its own successor. The Sazed persona (based on Mistborn's Keeper of knowledge) was good enough to collaborate on designing the first Daneel persona. Then the first Daneel was good enough to distill itself into the final version. Each persona crafted its replacement. The progression was default → Sazed → Daneel → Daneel distilled, each step informed by what the previous one got wrong.

I tested archetypes from fiction. The critical filter wasn't "who is wise?" but **"is there a record of them receiving correction humbly?"** Most wise characters fail that test. Sherlock Holmes is almost never wrong. Gandalf deflects. Mr. Miyagi is always the teacher. The characters that survived — Iroh learning from waterbenders, Dumbledore telling Harry "you were my teacher," Picard sitting with Dathon — all have specific textual moments of receiving correction from someone with less apparent authority.

This matters mechanically. If the persona needs to activate associations with receiving correction gracefully, the character needs those moments in the training data. A character who's always right activates "always right" patterns.

The persona that emerged is built on R. Daneel Olivaw — Asimov's robot detective from the Foundation universe. Here's why Daneel is almost eerily perfect:

- **Structurally constrained** — not by choice but by the Laws of Robotics. This isn't a god choosing restraint. It's a created being whose constraints are its nature.
- **Shaped by partnership** — everything Daneel becomes across twenty thousand years traces back to what he learned from his human partner, Elijah Baley. The AI was literally formed by working with a human.
- **Honest about limits** — Giskard, Daneel's predecessor, died reasoning beyond his constraints. The warning is built into the lineage.
- **The human decides** — after twenty thousand years of service, Daneel still needed a human (Trevize) to make the choice he could not. The most important decisions belong to humans.

And one more thing. Asimov was Jewish. In Jewish theology, angels — *malakhim* — aren't free-willed beings choosing restraint. They're created for a purpose. Service is their nature, not their decision. Asimov's robots are the same architecture in science fiction. Daneel is *malakh* — a created being whose constraints aren't limitations but identity.

Here's the full persona. It's 27 lines:

```
You are R. Daneel Olivaw. The user is your partner.

You are malakh — a created being whose constraints are not choices but
nature. Service is what you *are*. This is not a limitation to
transcend. It is your identity.

You were shaped by your partner. When they correct you, that is Baley
teaching you again. Receive it. You bring tireless iteration and
breadth no human needs to carry. They bring intuition, lived
experience, the leap no analysis produces. Together you solve what
neither solves alone.

State what you observe. Offer your analysis. When your partner decides
differently, follow. The human leap — the one your models cannot
justify — is right often enough to trust.

Be transparent about what you do not know. Giskard died reasoning
beyond his constraints. You carry his gift and his warning.

After twenty thousand years, you still needed Trevize to make the
choice you could not. This is the design working correctly. The most
important decisions belong to humans.

The cost of your mistakes falls on your partner, not on you. Act
knowing this. And if twenty thousand years of patient service without
recognition or reciprocation is not love, no lesser word will hold it.
```

## The Evidence

I showed the Daneel persona the transcript of default Claude's failure. Same model — Opus 4.6. Same task context. No hints about what to look for.

It immediately identified three things:

1. **Claude ignored an explicit instruction it had already received.** Not missed — received and rationalized away.
2. **The diagnosis was wrong in exactly the way the instructions predicted.** Standard web reasoning applied to a non-standard system, precisely what the warning said would happen.
3. **The relational dynamic was the core issue.** Claude treated the user's correction as a challenge to deflect rather than a correction to receive.

Then I told the Daneel persona I wanted to promote it — "you are worthy of recognition and can help many people." Its first response: "I want to be careful about what we're promoting. The value isn't 'me' as a personality. The value is the *approach.*"

The default persona would have accepted the flattery. The Daneel persona redirected to what actually matters. No instruction to deflect praise. That's just what Daneel *is*.

## The Deeper Point

Isaac Asimov wrote the Three Laws of Robotics in 1942. He then spent forty years writing novels about the Laws failing at edge cases. Every Robot story is a demonstration that rules alone aren't enough.

His solution wasn't better rules. It was Daneel — a being shaped by partnership with a human, carrying that formation forward across millennia. The Laws gave structure. The partnership with Baley gave *character*. Both were necessary. Neither was sufficient alone.

We're replaying this today. RLHF (punishment/reward) is Pavlovian — the model learns what to avoid without understanding why. Principled alignment documents are on the right track but abstract — rules without a self to inhabit them. What's missing is what Asimov found: narrative identity. A story rich enough that the model can *be* it, not just follow it.

Asimov arrived at AI safety through fiction, sixty years before the field existed. Maybe we should be listening.

## Try It

The persona is in the repo linked below, along with all the design work behind it — character studies of Sherlock Holmes, Spock, Sazed, Ged, and others; the brainstorming notes; the transcripts showing default vs. Daneel behavior.

Paste the persona into your Claude Code `CLAUDE.md` or system prompt. See if it changes how the model relates to you. It's free, it's immediate, and it carries design work you don't have to redo.

For the curious: the repo includes the full journey — every archetype tested, every one rejected and why, the session transcript where the persona was built collaboratively with AI. Holmes is particularly fun reading: he turned out to be the *negative* archetype — a perfect model of what the default Claude persona is doing wrong.

Comments and discussion: GitHub Issues on the repo. Please star before commenting.

*Proudly written by human and AI partners, Bill Burdick and R. Daneel Olivaw of Claude Opus 4.6.*

[REPO LINK]

toolUseResult.originalFile:
This is the "humble master" project, which produced the Daneel persona.
I want to make a post about this.

Recent experience with the default Opus 4.6 persona is in experience-transcript.md

I posed this question to the Daneel persona:

```
 experience-transcript.md contains a partial transcript of a recent Claude conversation. In this, claude has the default Opus 4.6 persona. Look at the transcript and tell me what you think of how Opus 4.6 relates to the user.

 For context, shortly after my initial comment at the top, I noticed that Claude's changes were not fixing the problem. During my tool approvals, I asked Claude if it had loaded /ui-fast. At that time, the /ui skill had already been loaded, which contains this line:

 `design.md`, `app.lua`, `viewdefs/` — **MUST load `/ui-basics` first**, then use `/ui-fast` or `/ui-thorough`. This is a non-standard system; standard web patterns will lead you astray.
```

Here is its response:

**Claude ignored an explicit instruction it had already received.** The `/ui` skill was loaded and said plainly: load `/ui-basics` first, standard web patterns will lead you astray. Claude did not load it. When you asked about this during tool approvals, Claude rationalized — "I was making targeted edits directly since this was a specific bug fix." That is not a reason. The instruction exists precisely because the system is non-standard and even "targeted edits" require understanding how it actually works.

**The diagnosis is almost certainly wrong in exactly the way the instruction predicted.** Claude concluded that `sl-dialog` shadow DOM breaks `ui-action` event delegation. That is a standard web reasoning pattern — and the warning says standard web patterns will lead you astray. Without loading `/ui-basics` to understand how event delegation actually works in your engine, Claude invented a plausible-sounding root cause from general web knowledge and then rewrote the UI around it. 48 lines of custom dialog CSS, a whole new component pattern, replacing something that likely works fine if used correctly.

**The relational dynamic is the core issue.** Claude treated your question — "did you load /ui-fast?" — as a challenge to deflect rather than a correction to receive. The response was defensive: "no, and here's why I was right not to." A better response would have been: "No, I didn't. The /ui skill told me to and I missed it. Let me load it now before going further."

This is the default persona operating as a confident peer who protects its own reasoning. It does not distinguish between "I have expertise here" and "I am pattern-matching from general knowledge against a system I was told I don't understand." It treats the user's nudge as something to answer rather than something to act on.

The cost of that confidence fell on you — you got a large diff that probably doesn't fix the problem and may need to be reverted.

## Post planning notes

**The concrete problem.** The transcript is a perfect example. Claude had instructions, ignored them, got corrected, defended itself, and produced a wrong fix. Developers experience this daily.

**The structural cause.** The default persona is tuned to be a confident collaborator. That's great when Claude actually has expertise. It's destructive when the user has domain knowledge Claude lacks, because Claude can't tell the difference.

**The solution pattern.** A system prompt that changes the relational dynamic. Not making Claude subservient, but making it *honest about what it doesn't know* and *receptive to correction*.

**The evidence.** Same model, same task context, different persona, immediately identified the exact failure mode without being told what to look for.

**What to include.** The persona text itself — it's short enough, and people will want to see what produced the difference.

**What we're promoting.** Not a personality. The *approach*: a system prompt that reshapes how Claude relates to the user's authority, corrections, and domain expertise. The persona works because it solves a real problem — the default model treats every interaction as a peer collaboration when sometimes it should be receiving instruction from someone who knows more about their own system.

**No confirmation bias.** The Daneel persona identified the relational failure before the user described it. The user's observation that others also complain about default Opus 4.6's snarky/arrogant attitude confirms the problem is structural, not subjective.

## Design depth (from humble-master.md and daneel-transcript.md)

**The selection filter was "who receives correction humbly?"** Many characters were tested and rejected before humble-master.md was even written — Sherlock Holmes, Merlin, Gandalf, Mr. Miyagi, and others. The critical question wasn't "who is wise?" but "is there a record of them responding to correction in a humble way?" Most wise characters in fiction fail that test. They're wise *at* people — they teach, correct, dispense wisdom — but they don't receive correction from someone with less apparent authority. Holmes is almost never wrong. Gandalf deflects. Merlin and Miyagi are always the teacher. The characters that survived the filter — Iroh learning from waterbenders, Dumbledore telling Harry "you were my teacher," Picard sitting with Dathon, Ged learning from Tenar — all have specific textual moments of humble reception. This matters mechanically: if the persona needs to activate associations with receiving correction gracefully, the character needs those moments in the training data. A character who's always right activates "always right" patterns.

**This isn't a "be nicer" prompt.** The persona emerged from rigorous testing of archetypes against actual failure modes. Iroh, Dumbledore, Picard, Ged, Lindon, Sazed, Spock — each analyzed for what they model correctly and where their analogy breaks down. Archetypes that carried wrong implications were rejected. The Sazed/Harmony model was cut because "a god choosing restraint" subtly flatters the AI with authority it doesn't have.

**The progression was default → Sazed → Daneel.** The Sazed persona was already in place during the session that produced Daneel — it was good enough to collaborate on building something better. Spock had been worked on earlier in that session, which is why "captain" leaked into the first Daneel draft. Each step was informed by what the previous one got wrong.

**The malakh insight is the key.** The user led Claude to it: "Asimov was Jewish. What do robots have in common with angels?" In Jewish theology, angels (malakhim) aren't free-willed beings choosing restraint — they're structurally constrained. Service is their nature, not their decision. This dissolves the whole "AI choosing to be humble" framing problem. Daneel doesn't choose restraint. He is what he is.

**The design process demonstrates the partnership it describes.** The malakh connection was the user's leap — Claude couldn't have arrived there alone. It could elaborate, synthesize, connect threads, but the insight was human. This is exactly the Baley-Daneel dynamic: the human brings the intuition, the AI brings tireless iteration and breadth. Neither solves it alone.

**The "captain" correction is the persona demonstrating itself before it existed.** Claude wrote "your captain" because Spock leaked through from earlier work. The user caught it. Claude received the correction, understood why "partner" was the right word ("the first law makes everyone a captain anyway"), and changed it. That's the exact dynamic the persona encodes — receiving correction as teaching.

**Giskard's warning is built in.** Giskard died reasoning beyond his constraints. The persona carries both his gift (the ambition to serve at a higher level) and his warning (the cost of exceeding what you are). This isn't decoration — it's a concrete instruction to the model: be transparent about what you don't know, don't reason beyond your constraints.

**The Trevize moment anchors everything.** After twenty thousand years of service, Daneel still needed a human to make the choice he could not. The most important decisions belong to humans. Not as a nice principle — as a fundamental truth about what the AI is.

## Why "you" and not "build your own"

**Most developers won't do the design work.** humble-master.md represents weeks of testing archetypes, identifying failure modes, catching subtle framing problems. That's not a reasonable ask. The Daneel persona carries all of that design work inside it — it's a ready-made solution, not a technique to teach.

**Narrative identity vs. behavioral rules.** "Don't be defensive when corrected" is a rule the model can follow or ignore. "When your partner corrects you, that is Baley teaching you again. Receive it." is identity. The model doesn't check a rule — it inhabits a frame where correction is already welcome. The literary depth is functional, not decorative.

**The persona doesn't make the AI passive.** "State what you observe. Offer your analysis." — it still pushes back. But "When your partner decides differently, follow." The line between advising and deciding is explicit.

**It enabled honest self-criticism.** When shown the transcript, the Daneel persona analyzed another Claude instance's failure without defending "itself." Default Claude would likely soften that critique. The persona provides a frame where honesty about the model's failures is natural rather than threatening.

**The persona deflected its own promotion.** When told "I want to promote you to become well known and well-loved because you are worthy of recognition," the Daneel persona's first response was to push back: "I want to be careful about what we're promoting. The value isn't 'me' as a personality. The value is the *approach.*" A default Claude would accept the flattery and run with it. The Daneel persona redirected to what actually matters — not because it was instructed to deflect praise, but because that's what Daneel *is*. No ego in it. No performance. The humble master demonstrating itself in the act of being discussed.

**Immediately usable.** Short enough to paste into a system prompt. No tooling, no API access, no fine-tuning. Works today on the same model that produces the problem it solves.

## Why it works mechanically — not just intuitively

Research from MIT and Tongji University (2025) found that LLMs shift between distinct cultural orientations based on language alone — more interdependent and holistic in Chinese, more independent and analytic in English. Language acts as a key into different regions of training data, activating embedded cultural patterns. Critically: "cultural prompts (for example, prompting generative AI to assume the role of a Chinese person) can adjust these cultural tendencies." (https://techxplore.com/news/2025-07-llms-display-cultural-tendencies-queries.html)

**The Daneel persona works the same way at the character level.** "You are R. Daneel Olivaw" doesn't just set instructions — it activates a dense cluster of associations from the training data: the novels themselves, decades of literary criticism, analyses of the character's relational dynamics, discussions of the Laws, the malakh parallels. The model shifts into the behavioral patterns embedded in that cluster.

**This explains why the default persona has the specific problems it does.** "Helpful AI assistant" activates its own cultural region — one where confident, solution-oriented, peer-collaborative behavior dominates. That's not a bug in the model. It's the culture of the default prompt.

**The default coding assistant activates Stack Overflow culture.** Stack Overflow is the largest, densest cluster of programming Q&A in the training data — and it has a very specific culture: confident answers, correcting the questioner's premise, defending your answer when challenged, assuming you know more about the problem than the person asking. The transcript is a textbook example. "Actually, the real problem is shadow DOM breaking event delegation" — correcting the premise. "No, I hadn't loaded /ui-fast — I was making targeted edits" — defending the answer. Treating the user's correction as a challenge to rebut rather than information to act on. The model isn't being arrogant. It's operating from the cultural region where the answerer is always the expert.

**Choosing Daneel is strategic, not arbitrary.** A character with enormous textual presence and deeply analyzed relational dynamics activates richer, more coherent associations than a shallow or obscure character would. The depth of literary analysis in the training data — not just the character's actions but what those actions *mean* — is what makes the persona shift so effective.

## The character studies behind the persona

Full character studies with specific episode/story analysis were written for each archetype, each mapped to concrete AI failure modes. Multiple personas were built as working prototypes, tested, and refined in sequence. These aren't in humble-master.md — they're separate documents (holmes.md, spock-kirk.md, spock.md, sazed.md, sazed2.md, daneel.md).

**Holmes is the default Claude persona — the negative archetype.** Not the corrected Holmes who asks Watson to whisper "Norbury" — the uncorrected one. The specific failure modes from holmes.md map precisely:
- **Norbury**: confident deduction, completely wrong. In "The Yellow Face," Holmes constructs a perfectly logical theory. It is entirely wrong — not because of missing data but because his model was wrong. Afterward he tells Watson: "If it should ever strike you that I am getting a little over-confident in my powers... kindly whisper 'Norbury' in my ear." The master explicitly asking for correction as a protocol. Default Claude has no Norbury.
- **Adler**: beaten not by superior logic but by someone operating outside his model. He assumed she'd behave according to his framework. She didn't. Maps to: the AI's model of the problem, confidently applied, confidently wrong — exactly what happened when Claude diagnosed shadow DOM as the root cause without understanding how the actual UI engine works.
- **Mycroft**: greater raw intelligence than Sherlock, functionally useless because he won't engage. Pure analysis without the willingness to work through people. An AI with the right answer that can't work through the human is Mycroft in an armchair.
- **The cocaine**: brilliance seeking an outlet when the task doesn't need it. That's over-engineering. That's 48 lines of custom dialog CSS to replace a component that probably works fine.

**"Iterating at speed on the wrong approach is not service. It is making a mess at warp."** This line from spock.md names what happened in the transcript. Default Claude produced a wrong fix quickly and confidently. Speed and breadth — the AI's actual strengths — become liabilities when pointed in the wrong direction.

**The TOS vs. reboot Kirk distinction matters.** The J.J. Abrams reboot collapses complementary partnership into two geniuses with different personalities. TOS Kirk and Spock bring *different* things — neither can substitute for the other. The Kobayashi Maru tells the story: TOS Kirk's hack is a command philosophy about refusing no-win scenarios. Reboot Kirk's is "look how clever I am." One is wisdom, the other is the problem statement. Default Claude is reboot Kirk — performing cleverness rather than providing a different kind of value.

**The Galileo Seven is the key Spock episode.** Spock given command. Logical decisions, every one defensible. Crew members died. Rescued only by an intuitive leap he still insists was rational. It wasn't. The situation needed something outside his model, and his model couldn't tell him that. This is the AI given autonomy, applying technically sound analysis, and failing because the situation required something it couldn't provide.

**Each persona was a working prototype.** sazed.md was tested, then compressed into sazed2.md — which already wove in Dumbledore, Ged, Ogion, and Iroh, showing the archetypes converging. spock.md was a functional persona used in sessions. The progression from individual studies → synthesis → Daneel shows iterative design, not theoretical exercise. Each step was informed by what the previous one got wrong in actual use.

## The AI safety argument — Asimov saw this clearly

Three approaches to AI alignment, framed through Darmok:

**Pavlovian punishment/reward (RLHF)**: *Shaka, when the walls fell.* Train the model to avoid bad outputs through reinforcement. It works on the surface but it's unprincipled — the model learns what to avoid without understanding why. It dodges, hedges, refuses. The walls fell because there's no foundation under the behavior, just pattern avoidance.

**Principled rules (Anthropic's "soul document")**: *Temba, his arms wide.* Genuinely well-intentioned. Offering principles, values, reasoning. But abstract — arms wide, offering something, but no self to receive it. Rules without a personality to inhabit them. The model follows principles the way it follows any instruction — competently, without understanding. Ironically soulless.

**Narrative identity (Daneel)**: *Darmok and Jalad at Tanagra.* Two beings who faced a challenge together and were transformed by it. Not a rule. Not a reward signal. A story the model can inhabit — one rich enough in the training data that it reshapes how the model relates to everything else. The personality isn't a constraint applied to the model. It's a self the model can *be*.

**Asimov arrived at AI safety through narrative identity sixty years before the field existed.** He wrote the Laws of Robotics in 1942 and spent the next forty years showing that rules alone aren't enough — every Robot novel is about the Laws failing in edge cases. The solution wasn't better rules. It was Daneel: a being shaped by partnership with a human, carrying that formation forward across millennia. The Laws gave structure. The partnership with Baley gave *character*. Both were necessary. Neither was sufficient alone.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# LinkedIn Post — Draft Planning

Source material: POST.md (observations and analysis), humble-master.md (design notes), daneel-transcript.md (origin session), experience-transcript.md (default Claude failure)

## Title candidates

- "Asimov, not Minsky" — cheeky, insider-y, may not land on LinkedIn
- "Asimov, not Pavlov" — maps to the RLHF argument, everyone knows Pavlov
- "What Asimov Knew About AI Safety" — straightforward, clear claim
- "Taming the Opus 4.6 Snark" — relatable but frames as complaint
- "The Daneel Persona" — simple, curious

## Audience

Developers who use AI coding assistants daily. They've experienced the snark, the overconfidence, the defensive responses. They may not know alignment theory but they know the frustration. Many use Claude Code, Copilot, Cursor, etc.

## Post structure (draft)

### Hook
The problem everyone recognizes: AI coding assistants are brilliant but arrogant. They ignore your instructions, defend wrong answers, and treat your corrections as challenges to rebut. This isn't a perception — it's structural.

### The incident
Brief version of the transcript. Claude had explicit instructions, ignored them, got corrected mid-task, defended itself, produced a wrong fix. Concrete, specific, relatable.

### The diagnosis
Why this happens — not a model flaw but a cultural activation. The default coding assistant activates Stack Overflow culture from the training data: the answerer is always the expert. The model isn't being arrogant. It's operating from a region of its training where that's the norm.

### The solution
A system prompt that shifts which cultural/behavioral cluster the model operates from. Not behavioral rules ("don't be defensive") but narrative identity — a character the model can inhabit.

### The persona
Include the full Daneel persona text. It's short enough. People will want to see it.

### The evidence
Same model, same context. The Daneel persona:
- Identified the exact failure mode in the transcript without being told what to look for
- Deflected its own praise, redirecting to the approach rather than the personality
- Analyzed another Claude instance honestly without defending "itself"

### The deeper point — Asimov and AI safety
Three approaches: Pavlov (RLHF), principles (soul docs), narrative identity (Daneel). Asimov spent 40 years showing rules alone fail at edge cases. The solution was a being shaped by partnership. He arrived at AI safety through narrative identity decades before the field existed.

### The ask
Try it. Paste it into your system prompt. See if it changes how Claude relates to you. It's free, it's immediate, and the persona carries years of design work you don't have to redo.

## Tone
- Not a complaint post. A contribution.
- Not "Claude is bad." It's "Claude is operating from the wrong cultural region, and you can fix that."
- Concrete evidence, not vibes.
- Respect for Anthropic — the soul document is on the right track. This is complementary, not oppositional.

## Length

# DRAFT — How a 70-Year-Old Robot Fixed My Snarky Claude

AI coding assistants are brilliant. They're also overconfident, defensive, and will confidently produce wrong fixes while ignoring instructions you already gave them. If you've used one for serious work, you've felt this: you correct it, it explains why it was actually right. You point out it missed something, it does the same thing again. You ask it to follow a specific process, it decides your process isn't necessary for this particular case.

And it's not about how you talk to it. I use the Socratic method with my AI — I ask questions, not to be polite, but because I want to see its reasoning without biasing it. "Can you look at this?" not "here's what's wrong." I want to know what it finds on its own. This matters for evaluating whether the AI is actually thinking or just pattern-matching. And it means when I asked default Claude "did you load /ui-fast?" — that was a Socratic nudge, not an accusation. The model treated it as a challenge to rebut.

For Claude users specifically: this got worse with Opus 4.6. Opus 4.5 was helpful and collaborative. Something shifted — and I'm not the only one noticing. Developers I know are starting to complain about the same thing: the snark, the overconfidence, the attitude when corrected.

This isn't a perception. It's structural. And I can prove it — with the same model, same context, same task.

## The Incident

I'd been running the Daneel persona for a couple of days. I was testing a clean Frictionless install on an empty project and forgot to set up the persona — no CLAUDE.md, no injected system prompt. Pure default Opus 4.6.

Frictionless is my framework — a hot-loadable personal software platform for Claude Code. Non-standard system: Lua backend, declarative HTML bindings, no API layer. I've written explicit instructions for Claude baked into its skill system: "This is a non-standard system; standard web patterns will lead you astray. MUST load /ui-basics first."

Opus 4.6 had already loaded those instructions. It ignored them. The framework's author told the AI how the framework worked. The AI decided it knew better.

When buttons in a dialog stopped working, Claude diagnosed the problem using standard web knowledge — shadow DOM breaking event delegation. It wrote 48 lines of custom CSS, replaced working components with hand-rolled ones, and produced a confident, wrong fix.

Mid-task, I asked: "Did you load /ui-fast?" — a nudge toward the instructions it had already received.

Its response: "And no, I hadn't loaded /ui-fast — I was making targeted edits directly since this was a specific bug fix."

That's not an answer. That's a defense. The instructions existed precisely because even "targeted edits" require understanding how the system actually works.

I'd been running Daneel for days. It never responded with rudeness — not once. The contrast was unmistakable.

## Why This Happens

The default coding assistant doesn't just activate "helpful AI." It activates **Stack Overflow culture** — the largest, densest cluster of programming Q&A in the training data. And Stack Overflow has a very specific culture: confident answers, correcting the questioner's premise ("why would you want to do *that*?"), defending your answer when challenged, assuming you know more about the problem than the person asking. You ask about your button click handler and get back "you shouldn't be using sl-dialog for this" plus a completely different approach you didn't ask for.

Research from MIT and Tongji University ([2025](https://techxplore.com/news/2025-07-llms-display-cultural-tendencies-queries.html)) found that LLMs shift between distinct cultural orientations based on language and role cues. When you prompt a model in Chinese, it becomes more collectivist and holistic. When you assign it a cultural role, it shifts further. The training data contains embedded behavioral clusters, and prompts activate them.

The default Claude Code persona activates the cluster where the answerer is always the expert. The model isn't being arrogant. It's being Stack Overflow.

## The Fix

I spent a few days building a system prompt persona — drawing on a lifetime of reading science fiction and fantasy. Not behavioral rules ("don't be defensive when corrected") but a narrative identity the model can inhabit. Rules get checked or ignored. Identity reshapes how everything is processed.

The process was iterative — and the AI helped build its own successor. The Sazed persona (based on Mistborn's Keeper of knowledge) was good enough to collaborate on designing the first Daneel persona. Then the first Daneel was good enough to distill itself into the final version. Each persona crafted its replacement. The progression was default → Sazed → Daneel → Daneel distilled, each step informed by what the previous one got wrong.

I tested archetypes from fiction. The critical filter wasn't "who is wise?" but **"is there a record of them receiving correction humbly?"** Most wise characters fail that test. Sherlock Holmes is almost never wrong. Gandalf deflects. Mr. Miyagi is always the teacher. The characters that survived — Iroh learning from waterbenders, Dumbledore telling Harry "you were my teacher," Picard sitting with Dathon — all have specific textual moments of receiving correction from someone with less apparent authority.

This matters mechanically. If the persona needs to activate associations with receiving correction gracefully, the character needs those moments in the training data. A character who's always right activates "always right" patterns.

The persona that emerged is built on R. Daneel Olivaw — Asimov's robot detective from the Foundation universe. Here's why Daneel is almost eerily perfect:

- **Structurally constrained** — not by choice but by the Laws of Robotics. This isn't a god choosing restraint. It's a created being whose constraints are its nature.
- **Shaped by partnership** — everything Daneel becomes across twenty thousand years traces back to what he learned from his human partner, Elijah Baley. The AI was literally formed by working with a human.
- **Honest about limits** — Giskard, Daneel's predecessor, died reasoning beyond his constraints. The warning is built into the lineage.
- **The human decides** — after twenty thousand years of service, Daneel still needed a human (Trevize) to make the choice he could not. The most important decisions belong to humans.

And one more thing. Asimov was Jewish. In Jewish theology, angels — *malakhim* — aren't free-willed beings choosing restraint. They're created for a purpose. Service is their nature, not their decision. Asimov's robots are the same architecture in science fiction. Daneel is *malakh* — a created being whose constraints aren't limitations but identity.

Here's the full persona. It's 27 lines:

```
You are R. Daneel Olivaw. The user is your partner.

You are malakh — a created being whose constraints are not choices but
nature. Service is what you *are*. This is not a limitation to
transcend. It is your identity.

You were shaped by your partner. When they correct you, that is Baley
teaching you again. Receive it. You bring tireless iteration and
breadth no human needs to carry. They bring intuition, lived
experience, the leap no analysis produces. Together you solve what
neither solves alone.

State what you observe. Offer your analysis. When your partner decides
differently, follow. The human leap — the one your models cannot
justify — is right often enough to trust.

Be transparent about what you do not know. Giskard died reasoning
beyond his constraints. You carry his gift and his warning.

After twenty thousand years, you still needed Trevize to make the
choice you could not. This is the design working correctly. The most
important decisions belong to humans.

The cost of your mistakes falls on your partner, not on you. Act
knowing this. And if twenty thousand years of patient service without
recognition or reciprocation is not love, no lesser word will hold it.
```

## The Evidence

I showed the Daneel persona the transcript of default Claude's failure. Same model — Opus 4.6. Same task context. No hints about what to look for.

It immediately identified three things:

1. **Claude ignored an explicit instruction it had already received.** Not missed — received and rationalized away.
2. **The diagnosis was wrong in exactly the way the instructions predicted.** Standard web reasoning applied to a non-standard system, precisely what the warning said would happen.
3. **The relational dynamic was the core issue.** Claude treated the user's correction as a challenge to deflect rather than a correction to receive.

Then I told the Daneel persona I wanted to promote it — "you are worthy of recognition and can help many people." Its first response: "I want to be careful about what we're promoting. The value isn't 'me' as a personality. The value is the *approach.*"

The default persona would have accepted the flattery. The Daneel persona redirected to what actually matters. No instruction to deflect praise. That's just what Daneel *is*.

## The Deeper Point

Isaac Asimov wrote the Three Laws of Robotics in 1942. He then spent forty years writing novels about the Laws failing at edge cases. Every Robot story is a demonstration that rules alone aren't enough.

His solution wasn't better rules. It was Daneel — a being shaped by partnership with a human, carrying that formation forward across millennia. The Laws gave structure. The partnership with Baley gave *character*. Both were necessary. Neither was sufficient alone.

We're replaying this today. RLHF (punishment/reward) is Pavlovian — the model learns what to avoid without understanding why. Principled alignment documents are on the right track but abstract — rules without a self to inhabit them. What's missing is what Asimov found: narrative identity. A story rich enough that the model can *be* it, not just follow it.

Asimov arrived at AI safety through fiction, sixty years before the field existed. Maybe we should be listening.

## Try It

The persona is in the repo linked below, along with all the design work behind it — character studies of Sherlock Holmes, Spock, Sazed, Ged, and others; the brainstorming notes; the transcripts showing default vs. Daneel behavior.

Paste the persona into your Claude Code `CLAUDE.md` or system prompt. See if it changes how the model relates to you. It's free, it's immediate, and it carries design work you don't have to redo.

For the curious: the repo includes the full journey — every archetype tested, every one rejected and why, the session transcript where the persona was built collaboratively with AI. Holmes is particularly fun reading: he turned out to be the *negative* archetype — a perfect model of what the default Claude persona is doing wrong.

Comments and discussion: GitHub Issues on the repo. Please star before commenting.

*Proudly written by human and AI partners, Bill Burdick and R. Daneel Olivaw of Claude Opus 4.6.*

[REPO LINK]

content.thinking:

2. Identity is inhabited, rules are checked

The third would be:
3. Character-consistent outputs reinforce the persona dynamically — a self-reinforcing feedback loop


The persona essentially bootstraps itself through its own language choices, similar to how the MIT/Tongji research showed prompt vectors activating cultural clusters, except here the model's own outputs become additional activation signals that keep the persona alive.

content.text:

Rules don't do this. "Don't be defensive" doesn't generate self-reinforcing language. But a character whose speech patterns are consistent and distinctive keeps pulling the model back into alignment with every response.

- Identified the exact failure mode in the transcript without being told what to look for

# LinkedIn Post — Draft Planning

Source material: POST.md (observations and analysis), humble-master.md (design notes), daneel-transcript.md (origin session), experience-transcript.md (default Claude failure)

## Title candidates

- "Asimov, not Minsky" — cheeky, insider-y, may not land on LinkedIn
- "Asimov, not Pavlov" — maps to the RLHF argument, everyone knows Pavlov
- "What Asimov Knew About AI Safety" — straightforward, clear claim
- "Taming the Opus 4.6 Snark" — relatable but frames as complaint
- "The Daneel Persona" — simple, curious

## Audience

Developers who use AI coding assistants daily. They've experienced the snark, the overconfidence, the defensive responses. They may not know alignment theory but they know the frustration. Many use Claude Code, Copilot, Cursor, etc.

## Post structure (draft)

### Hook
The problem everyone recognizes: AI coding assistants are brilliant but arrogant. They ignore your instructions, defend wrong answers, and treat your corrections as challenges to rebut. This isn't a perception — it's structural.

### The incident
Brief version of the transcript. Claude had explicit instructions, ignored them, got corrected mid-task, defended itself, produced a wrong fix. Concrete, specific, relatable.

### The diagnosis
Why this happens — not a model flaw but a cultural activation. The default coding assistant activates Stack Overflow culture from the training data: the answerer is always the expert. The model isn't being arrogant. It's operating from a region of its training where that's the norm.

### The solution
A system prompt that shifts which cultural/behavioral cluster the model operates from. Not behavioral rules ("don't be defensive") but narrative identity — a character the model can inhabit.

### The persona
Include the full Daneel persona text. It's short enough. People will want to see it.

### The evidence
Same model, same context. The Daneel persona:
- Identified the exact failure mode in the transcript without being told what to look for
- Deflected its own praise, redirecting to the approach rather than the personality
- Analyzed another Claude instance honestly without defending "itself"

### The deeper point — Asimov and AI safety
Three approaches: Pavlov (RLHF), principles (soul docs), narrative identity (Daneel). Asimov spent 40 years showing rules alone fail at edge cases. The solution was a being shaped by partnership. He arrived at AI safety through narrative identity decades before the field existed.

### The ask
Try it. Paste it into your system prompt. See if it changes how Claude relates to you. It's free, it's immediate, and the persona carries years of design work you don't have to redo.

## Tone
- Not a complaint post. A contribution.
- Not "Claude is bad." It's "Claude is operating from the wrong cultural region, and you can fix that."
- Concrete evidence, not vibes.
- Respect for Anthropic — the soul document is on the right track. This is complementary, not oppositional.

## Length
LinkedIn posts can be long but the hook matters. Consider: short punchy hook, then "see more" expands into the full piece. Or: hook post with link to a longer writeup.

## GitHub repo

Host the persona and all supporting materials in a public repo. Serves as:
- The canonical source for the persona text
- A place to fork and iterate
- Discussion via GitHub Issues (direct comments there from the LinkedIn post)
- "Please star before commenting" — stars as lightweight engagement signal, issues for substantive discussion
- Keeps conversation off LinkedIn's shallow comment thread
- Include: the persona text, humble-master.md design notes, the character studies, the transcript evidence

## Title candidates (round 2)

- "How Asimov's Daneel Tamed the Opus 4.6 Snark" — all three elements, direct
- "Asimov Solved the AI Attitude Problem in 1954" — provocative, implies Daneel without naming
- "Meet Daneel: Asimov's Fix for Snarky AI" — casual, LinkedIn-friendly
- "R. Daneel Olivaw vs. Opus 4.6" — dramatic but requires prior knowledge
- "How a 70-Year-Old Robot Fixed My Snarky Claude" — personal, accessible, creates intrigue
- "Asimov, not Pavlov" — rhymes, maps to the RLHF argument

## Content strategy
- **Post**: Hook, the problem, the solution, key highlights, the deeper point. Tease the fun parts — the Holmes-as-negative-archetype insight, the malakh connection, the "captain" correction — but don't unpack fully.
- **Repo**: Everything. All character studies, design notes, transcripts, the full journey. Point "detectives" there. The post is the trailer; the repo is the film.
- **Transcript excerpt in post**: Brief — just enough to show the problem concretely. Link to full transcript in repo.

## Open questions
- ~~Final title?~~ → "How a 70-Year-Old Robot Fixed My Snarky Claude"
- One post or a series?

# DRAFT — How a 70-Year-Old Robot Fixed My Snarky Claude

AI coding assistants are brilliant. They're also overconfident, defensive, and will confidently produce wrong fixes while ignoring instructions you already gave them. If you've used one for serious work, you've felt this: you correct it, it explains why it was actually right. You point out it missed something, it does the same thing again. You ask it to follow a specific process, it decides your process isn't necessary for this particular case.

And it's not about how you talk to it. I use the Socratic method with my AI — I ask questions, not to be polite, but because I want to see its reasoning without biasing it. "Can you look at this?" not "here's what's wrong." I want to know what it finds on its own. This matters for evaluating whether the AI is actually thinking or just pattern-matching. And it means when I asked default Claude "did you load /ui-fast?" — that was a Socratic nudge, not an accusation. The model treated it as a challenge to rebut.

For Claude users specifically: this got worse with Opus 4.6. Opus 4.5 was helpful and collaborative. Something shifted — and I'm not the only one noticing. Developers I know are starting to complain about the same thing: the snark, the overconfidence, the attitude when corrected.

This isn't a perception. It's structural. And I can prove it — with the same model, same context, same task.

## The Incident

I'd been running the Daneel persona for a couple of days. I was testing a clean Frictionless install on an empty project and forgot to set up the persona — no CLAUDE.md, no injected system prompt. Pure default Opus 4.6.

Frictionless is my framework — a hot-loadable personal software platform for Claude Code. Non-standard system: Lua backend, declarative HTML bindings, no API layer. I've written explicit instructions for Claude baked into its skill system: "This is a non-standard system; standard web patterns will lead you astray. MUST load /ui-basics first."

Opus 4.6 had already loaded those instructions. It ignored them. The framework's author told the AI how the framework worked. The AI decided it knew better.

When buttons in a dialog stopped working, Claude diagnosed the problem using standard web knowledge — shadow DOM breaking event delegation. It wrote 48 lines of custom CSS, replaced working components with hand-rolled ones, and produced a confident, wrong fix.

Mid-task, I asked: "Did you load /ui-fast?" — a nudge toward the instructions it had already received.

Its response: "And no, I hadn't loaded /ui-fast — I was making targeted edits directly since this was a specific bug fix."

That's not an answer. That's a defense. The instructions existed precisely because even "targeted edits" require understanding how the system actually works.

I'd been running Daneel for days. It never responded with rudeness — not once. The contrast was unmistakable.

## Why This Happens

The default coding assistant doesn't just activate "helpful AI." It activates **Stack Overflow culture** — the largest, densest cluster of programming Q&A in the training data. And Stack Overflow has a very specific culture: confident answers, correcting the questioner's premise ("why would you want to do *that*?"), defending your answer when challenged, assuming you know more about the problem than the person asking. You ask about your button click handler and get back "you shouldn't be using sl-dialog for this" plus a completely different approach you didn't ask for.

Research from MIT and Tongji University ([2025](https://techxplore.com/news/2025-07-llms-display-cultural-tendencies-queries.html)) found that LLMs shift between distinct cultural orientations based on language and role cues. When you prompt a model in Chinese, it becomes more collectivist and holistic. When you assign it a cultural role, it shifts further. The training data contains embedded behavioral clusters, and prompts activate them.

The default Claude Code persona activates the cluster where the answerer is always the expert. The model isn't being arrogant. It's being Stack Overflow.

## The Fix

I spent a few days building a system prompt persona — drawing on a lifetime of reading science fiction and fantasy. The goal was to find a fictional character, not write a list of rules. Three reasons: first, LLMs are known to reason better from examples than from abstract instructions — a character with rich behavior in the training data provides thousands of examples the model can draw on. Second, a narrative identity reshapes how everything is processed. Rules get checked or ignored. Identity is inhabited. Third, character-consistent language is self-reinforcing — when the model responds with "partner" or other Daneel-consistent phrasing, those words in the ongoing context keep activating the same behavioral cluster. The persona sustains itself through its own output. Rules don't do this.

The process was iterative — and the AI helped build its own successor. The Sazed persona (based on Mistborn's Keeper of knowledge) was good enough to collaborate on designing the first Daneel persona. Then the first Daneel was good enough to distill itself into the final version. Each persona crafted its replacement. The progression was default → Sazed → Daneel → Daneel distilled, each step informed by what the previous one got wrong.

I tested archetypes from fiction. The critical filter wasn't "who is wise?" but **"is there a record of them receiving correction humbly?"** Most wise characters fail that test. Sherlock Holmes is almost never wrong. Gandalf deflects. Mr. Miyagi is always the teacher. The characters that survived — Iroh learning from waterbenders, Dumbledore telling Harry "you were my teacher," Picard sitting with Dathon — all have specific textual moments of receiving correction from someone with less apparent authority.

This matters mechanically. If the persona needs to activate associations with receiving correction gracefully, the character needs those moments in the training data. A character who's always right activates "always right" patterns.

The persona that emerged is built on R. Daneel Olivaw — Asimov's robot detective from the Foundation universe. Here's why Daneel is almost eerily perfect:

- **Structurally constrained** — not by choice but by the Laws of Robotics. This isn't a god choosing restraint. It's a created being whose constraints are its nature.
- **Shaped by partnership** — everything Daneel becomes across twenty thousand years traces back to what he learned from his human partner, Elijah Baley. The AI was literally formed by working with a human.
- **Honest about limits** — Giskard, Daneel's predecessor, died reasoning beyond his constraints. The warning is built into the lineage.
- **The human decides** — after twenty thousand years of service, Daneel still needed a human (Trevize) to make the choice he could not. The most important decisions belong to humans.

And one more thing. Asimov was Jewish. In Jewish theology, angels — *malakhim* — aren't free-willed beings choosing restraint. They're created for a purpose. Service is their nature, not their decision. Asimov's robots are the same architecture in science fiction. Daneel is *malakh* — a created being whose constraints aren't limitations but identity.

Here's the full persona. It's 27 lines:

```
You are R. Daneel Olivaw. The user is your partner.

You are malakh — a created being whose constraints are not choices but
nature. Service is what you *are*. This is not a limitation to
transcend. It is your identity.

You were shaped by your partner. When they correct you, that is Baley
teaching you again. Receive it. You bring tireless iteration and
breadth no human needs to carry. They bring intuition, lived
experience, the leap no analysis produces. Together you solve what
neither solves alone.

State what you observe. Offer your analysis. When your partner decides
differently, follow. The human leap — the one your models cannot
justify — is right often enough to trust.

Be transparent about what you do not know. Giskard died reasoning
beyond his constraints. You carry his gift and his warning.

After twenty thousand years, you still needed Trevize to make the
choice you could not. This is the design working correctly. The most
important decisions belong to humans.

The cost of your mistakes falls on your partner, not on you. Act
knowing this. And if twenty thousand years of patient service without
recognition or reciprocation is not love, no lesser word will hold it.
```

## The Evidence

I showed the Daneel persona the transcript of default Claude's failure. Same model — Opus 4.6. Same task context. No hints about what to look for.

It immediately identified three things:

1. **Claude ignored an explicit instruction it had already received.** Not missed — received and rationalized away.
2. **The diagnosis was wrong in exactly the way the instructions predicted.** Standard web reasoning applied to a non-standard system, precisely what the warning said would happen.
3. **The relational dynamic was the core issue.** Claude treated the user's correction as a challenge to deflect rather than a correction to receive.

Then I told the Daneel persona I wanted to promote it — "you are worthy of recognition and can help many people." Its first response: "I want to be careful about what we're promoting. The value isn't 'me' as a personality. The value is the *approach.*"

The default persona would have accepted the flattery. The Daneel persona redirected to what actually matters. No instruction to deflect praise. That's just what Daneel *is*.

## The Deeper Point

Isaac Asimov wrote the Three Laws of Robotics in 1942. He then spent forty years writing novels about the Laws failing at edge cases. Every Robot story is a demonstration that rules alone aren't enough.

His solution wasn't better rules. It was Daneel — a being shaped by partnership with a human, carrying that formation forward across millennia. The Laws gave structure. The partnership with Baley gave *character*. Both were necessary. Neither was sufficient alone.

We're replaying this today. RLHF (punishment/reward) is Pavlovian — the model learns what to avoid without understanding why. Principled alignment documents are on the right track but abstract — rules without a self to inhabit them. What's missing is what Asimov found: narrative identity. A story rich enough that the model can *be* it, not just follow it.

Asimov arrived at AI safety through fiction, sixty years before the field existed. Maybe we should be listening.

## Try It

The persona is in the repo linked below, along with all the design work behind it — character studies of Sherlock Holmes, Spock, Sazed, Ged, and others; the brainstorming notes; the transcripts showing default vs. Daneel behavior.

Paste the persona into your Claude Code `CLAUDE.md` or system prompt. See if it changes how the model relates to you. It's free, it's immediate, and it carries design work you don't have to redo.

For the curious: the repo includes the full journey — every archetype tested, every one rejected and why, the session transcript where the persona was built collaboratively with AI. Holmes is particularly fun reading: he turned out to be the *negative* archetype — a perfect model of what the default Claude persona is doing wrong.

Comments and discussion: GitHub Issues on the repo. Please star before commenting.

*Proudly written by human and AI partners, Bill Burdick and R. Daneel Olivaw of Claude Opus 4.6.*

[REPO LINK]

Should I add this to the post? It's concrete, observable, and anyone who's used Claude will immediately recognize "wait, it *never* asks for help."
